turns.content: Hey, I've read about error handling in Rust iterators before, but I'm still a bit confused. Specifically, when collecting an iterator of `Result<T, E>`, what's the best practice?

Good question! When you iterate over a collection of `Result<T, E>` and want to collect it back into either a `Vec<Result<T, E>>` or an aggregated result like `Result<Vec<T>, E>`, there are some common pitfalls beginners often hit. What do you think the main issue is?
I'm not sure. I thought maybe it would just collect all successes and ignore any errors, but then I realized that might cause problems if one error ruins everything.

Exactly! When you use `iter.collect::<Result<Vec<T>, E>>()`, Rust's iterator will short-circuit on the first error. If an item is `Err(e)`, it returns that error immediately, discarding any remaining successes. This can be surprising if you expect to collect all results.
Oh wait, so I can't just collect into a vector of results?

Right! You can collect `iter.collect::<Vec<Result<T, E>>>()`. This way, you keep every item as is, regardless of whether it's success or error. That lets you later analyze all the successes and failures separately.
What if I want to separate successes from errors then?

Good point! You can use `partition_result` (available in nightly Rust) or write a custom function that splits the iterator into two vectors: one with all successes and another with all errors. This is especially useful if you want to handle them differently.
That makes sense. And for propagating inner results, which is better?

If you have an iterator of `Result<Iterator<Item=Result<T, E>>, E>`, using `flat_map` can flatten out all the nested iterators into a single iterator of `Result<T, E>`. This way, you get one stream to process.
So, should I always collect into `Vec<Result<T, E>>` then?

That depends on your use case. If you want all errors and successes in one pass, definitely go with `Vec<Result<T, E>>`. But if you care about performance or handling failures early, collecting into a `Result<Vec<T>, Vec<E>>` can be more efficient.
Hmm, I see. So in my main function, using `iter.collect::<Result<Vec<T>, E>>()?` is risky?

Yes! That will short-circuit immediately on the first error. If you want to handle all results and decide later whether to propagate an error or not, better collect into a `Vec<Result<T, E>>`. Then you can use additional logic to aggregate or propagate errors as needed.
Got it! So I should avoid collecting directly into a result if I care about all items?
Yes, that's the safest bet. It avoids unexpected short-circuits and lets you be explicit in your error handling strategy.

Hey, I'm a Java dev trying to prep for my Rust interview and I keep getting confused about how to handle a stream of errors. In Java I'd just catch exceptions in a loop, but here it seems like `iter.collect::<Result<Vec<T>, E>>()` stops at the first failure? Is that right?

You nailed the behavior there. When you call `iter.collect::<Result<Vec<T>, E>>()`, the iterator short-circuits on the first error and returns a single `Err` value instead of collecting all failures into a vector. If you want to keep going, you need something like `partition_result` which is available on nightly Rust now, or you can use `flat_map` if your items are already nested results.
Got it for short-circuiting. But what if I actually want to split the successes and failures into two separate vectors? Can I do that with standard stable Rust or is `partition_result` required?

On stable Rust you can't do a true split in one call without extra crates, but there's a common pattern using `fold` with an accumulator tuple. You'll carry two vectors around and push successes to one and errors to the other as you iterate. If your target is nightly or later, then `partition_result` becomes much simpler because it returns `(Vec<T>, Vec<E>)` directly.
I see. So if I'm using nightly features, does `partition_result` handle the types cleanly? And what about performance differences between a vector of results versus a result containing a vector?

Exactly, on nightly `partition_result` gives you two vectors upfront which is very clean. Performance-wise, a `Vec<Result<T,E>>` stores every item regardless of whether it succeeded or failed, so memory usage grows with the full stream size. A `Result<Vec<T>, E>` only holds the successful items once an error occurs before that point, making it more compact but losing all later errors.
That makes sense for trade-offs. But what if I'm doing a flat map scenario where each item is already `Result`? Would `flat_map` propagate those inner results correctly?

Yes, `flat_map` works great there. If your iterator yields `Option<T>` or nested `Result`s, calling `iter.flat_map(|x| x?)` will unwrap the inner success and collect them all into a single vector while treating any `Err` as a hard stop unless you restructure with `partition_result`. It's basically chaining unwraps in one expression.
Okay, so in main I'd just use `into_iter().collect::<Result<Vec<T>, E>>()` and let the compiler handle the propagation? What happens if I mix that with manual error handling?

In `main` you can call `iter.into_iter().collect::<Result<Vec<T>, E>>()` and then match on the result. If you want more control, you'd manually collect into a temporary `Vec<Result<T,E>>`, then either use `partition_result` or convert to `Result<Vec<T>, Vec<E>>`. Mixing manual handling with `collect` usually means you're building up state before deciding whether to short-circuit.
Thanks for the breakdown. So if I'm stuck between a vector of errors and a single error, when should I choose which approach?

Use `Result<Vec<T>, E>` when you only care about stopping on the first failure and want a clean error type. Pick `Vec<Result<T,E>>` or `partition_result` when you need to preserve every intermediate result for later analysis, logging, or retry logic. The choice depends entirely on whether your downstream needs all failures or just the first one.
